summary:
Panama declared independence from Colombia on November 3, 1903, a secession facilitated directly by the United States government. The separation was engineered in the context of American determination to build a canal across the isthmus. The Colombian Senate had rejected the Hay-Herrán Treaty that would have granted the US the right to construct the canal, and the administration of President Theodore Roosevelt then backed a Panamanian independence movement that had been simmering for decades.

US warships prevented Colombian troops from reaching Panama to suppress the revolt, and the new Republic of Panama was recognized by the United States within days. The Hay-Bunau-Varilla Treaty, signed just two weeks after independence, granted the United States control over the Canal Zone in perpetuity in exchange for ten million dollars and annual payments. Construction of the Panama Canal began in 1904 and was completed in 1914, transforming global maritime trade. The episode epitomized the interventionist dimension of US foreign policy in Latin America during the era of dollar diplomacy.
